title: Dog Torn Dew Claw Bleeding: First Aid and When to Call a Vet
meta_desc: Find out what to do if your dog's dew claw is torn and bleeding. Learn about first aid, urgency levels, and why a photo is vital for triage.
content_html:
Understanding Torn Dew Claws

A torn dew claw occurs when the nail located higher up on your dog's leg gets snagged on carpet, brush, or furniture. Because this nail is attached to a digit that contains a sensitive nerve and blood supply known as the quick, an injury here is often very painful and may result in persistent bleeding.

Urgency Level: Medium

A torn dew claw is classified as a medium urgency. While it is rarely life-threatening, it is an acutely painful condition that carries a high risk of infection if left untreated. You should seek veterinary care within 24 hours to have the damaged portion of the nail safely removed and to receive appropriate pain relief. If your dog is unable to walk or the bleeding cannot be stopped with pressure, seek immediate assistance.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear, well-lit photo of the injured nail is incredibly helpful for veterinary triage. A photo allows a professional to determine if the nail is completely detached, if the sensitive quick is exposed, or if there is swelling in the surrounding toe. This visual information helps the vet team decide how quickly your dog needs to be seen and can provide you with more specific first-aid instructions while you are en route.

faqs.question: How can I stop the bleeding at home?
faqs.answer: Apply gentle, firm pressure with a clean cloth for 5 to 10 minutes. If bleeding persists, you can apply styptic powder or cornstarch to the tip of the nail to help it clot.

faqs.question: Can I trim the broken nail myself?
faqs.answer: It is generally not recommended to trim a torn dew claw at home, as cutting into the quick is extremely painful and may cause the bleeding to restart or worsen. A vet can use local numbing or sedation to remove it safely.

faqs.question: Will my dog need medication for this?
faqs.answer: Most veterinarians will prescribe a short course of antibiotics to prevent infection and anti-inflammatory medication to manage the significant pain and swelling associated with the tear.
